description: The provided image is a user-interface button labeled "Check for updates" and does not depict any scientific material figure. Consequently, no subfigures, curves, spectral peaks, microstructural regions, coordinate axes, or scale bars can be identified from the image pixels. The accompanying text extraction summary reports that the paper "Strengthening mechanisms in Fe-Al based ferritic low-density steels" investigates Fe-5Al, Fe-6Al, Fe-6.8Al, and Fe-9Al alloys. Reported figures include engineering stress-strain curves for Fe-6.8Al in the 98% cold-rolled condition (yield strength ~1800 MPa), after annealing at 750 degrees Celsius for 5 minutes (yield strength ~700 MPa, ultimate tensile strength ~870 MPa, elongation 20.0%), and after 60 minutes (yield strength ~265 MPa, ultimate tensile strength ~394 MPa, elongation 29.8%). The summary further reports small-angle X-ray scattering data indicating Fe3Al DO3 ordered phase sizes of 2.1 to 3.7 nanometers, scanning and transmission electron microscopy images revealing transitions from elongated pancaked grains to equiaxed recrystallized grains, and X-ray diffraction patterns confirming body-centered-cubic ferrite with varying micro-strain. All material system, sample, processing, and property details are derived from the text extraction summary rather than from direct visual observation of the provided image.

This figure presents Mössbauer transmission spectra (left column) and the corresponding hyperfine magnetic field distributions P(Bhf) (right column) for α-Fe foil and ferritic low-density steels with increasing aluminum contents: Fe-5Al, Fe-6Al, Fe-6.8Al, and Fe-9Al. Panels (a, c, e, g, i) show velocity-dependent transmission spectra fitted with theoretical sextets (black lines) to experimental data (red markers), while panels (b, d, f, h, j) display the probability density of the hyperfine field Bhf, where the humps are deconvoluted into individual Gaussian-like components (green curves). With rising Al addition, the spectra progressively broaden and lose the symmetric six-line profile characteristic of α-Fe, and the Bhf distributions evolve from a dominant single peak near 33 T toward increasingly complex multipeak profiles with additional low-field components between approximately 20 T and 32 T. This systematic change reflects altered local atomic environments around Fe atoms and indicates the development of short-range order or nano-sized ordered phases, providing spectroscopic evidence for local structure evolution underlying the strengthening behavior in these steels.

Figure 2 presents 2D small-angle X-ray scattering (SAXS) intensity maps for four Fe-Al based ferritic low-density steels. Subfigures (a), (b), (c), and (d) correspond to powder samples of Fe-5Al, Fe-6Al, Fe-6.8Al, and Fe-9Al (wt%), respectively, which the text reports were prepared by filing followed by stress-relief annealing at 600 °C for 60 min in vacuum. Each map displays a radially symmetric pattern on a pixel-based detector, with a central blue core surrounded by concentric yellow-green rings indicating diffuse scattering from nano-sized ordered precipitates, and two horizontal black bands attributed to detector gaps. Visually, the scattering signal intensifies and broadens progressively from (a) to (d). The text interprets this trend as evidence that increasing Al content promotes the formation and growth of ordered Fe3Al (DO3) precipitates, with reported SAXS-derived sizes and volume fractions increasing from approximately 2.6 nm and 0.4% for Fe-5Al to about 3.7 nm and 1.9% for Fe-9Al. Collectively, these profiles provide direct structural evidence of nano-scale ordering in the ferrite matrix, underpinning the quantitative strengthening mechanism analysis in the study.

Figure 3 displays logarithmic small-angle X-ray scattering (SAXS) intensity as a function of the scattering vector q (nm⁻¹, logarithmic axis) for ferritic Fe-Al low-density steels with four compositions: Fe-5AL (open circles), Fe-6AL (open squares), Fe-6.8AL (open upward triangles), and Fe-9AL (open diamonds). Open symbols represent experimental data, while solid blue lines are fits using Eq. (3). The curves are vertically offset by a corresponding factor to avoid overlap, as noted in the caption. Broken dotted lines indicate a q⁻⁴ power-law dependence. The progression from lower to higher Al content shows changes in the low-q Guinier regime and overall scattering contrast, consistent with the presence of nano-sized ordered Fe₃Al (DO₃) domains whose size and volume fraction increase with Al addition. These SAXS results provide microstructural evidence linking the evolution of ordered precipitates to composition-dependent order-strengthening mechanisms in the investigated steels.

This dual-Y-axis plot (Fig. 4) reports the variation of diameter and volume fraction of the Fe3Al ordered phase as a function of Al content in Fe-Al based ferritic low-density steels. The horizontal axis indicates weight percentage of Al at discrete levels of 5, 6, 7, and 9 wt%. Solid circles (left Y-axis, Volume fraction, 0–3 %) represent the volume fraction of the ordered phase, while solid squares (right Y-axis, Diameter, 0–4 nm) denote its average diameter, both determined from small-angle X-ray scattering (SAXS) analysis with associated error bars. The volume fraction increases monotonically from approximately 0.4 % at 5 wt% Al to about 1.9 % at 9 wt% Al. Concurrently, the ordered-phase diameter rises from roughly 2.6 nm to about 3.7 nm across the same compositional range. These trends quantitatively demonstrate that increasing Al content promotes greater precipitation of nano-sized ordered Fe3Al (DO3) domains, supporting the core conclusion that ordered-phase strengthening serves as an additional strengthening mechanism in these alloys, consistent with accompanying TEM and Mössbauer characterization.

Figure 5 is a scanning electron microscopy (SEM) micrograph showing the surface morphology of Fe-6.8Al ferritic low-density steel after isothermal annealing at 750 °C for 60 min. The image displays equiaxed, fully recrystallized ferrite grains with visible grain boundaries appearing as bright intergranular networks against a darker matrix. The scale bar represents 300 μm. According to the accompanying text, this annealed condition exhibits substantial grain growth compared with the 5 min anneal at the same temperature (reported average grain sizes of ~48.3 ± 1.2 μm versus ~25.2 ± 5.0 μm), and stands in marked contrast to the 98% cold-rolled state that possessed heavily elongated, pancaked grains and shear bands oriented ~40° to the rolling direction. The micrograph thereby provides direct evidence that prolonged annealing drives recrystallization and subsequent grain coarsening in the BCC ferrite matrix, which the authors link to a significant reduction in yield strength governed by Hall-Petch grain-boundary strengthening.

This figure presents a dark-field TEM micrograph of the Fe-6.8Al ferritic low-density steel, showing the distribution of nano-sized ordered precipitates within the ferrite matrix. The main image captures bright contrast regions corresponding to very fine ordered domains, several of which are marked by red arrows, with a scale bar of 200 nm. The inset displays a selected-area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern along the [011] zone axis of the Fe-Al matrix, where intense spots arise from fundamental matrix reflections (e.g., 200, 2̄11, 01̄1) and weak superlattice spots highlighted by yellow arrows confirm the presence of an ordered phase. The dark-field image was formed using these weak 00 superlattice reflections, causing the ordered domains to appear bright against the darker matrix. Together, the micrograph and diffraction pattern provide direct evidence for nanoscale ordered Fe3Al (DO3) phase particles, approximately 2.1 ± 0.2 nm in size, thereby supporting the ordered-phase strengthening mechanism in this alloy system.

Figure 7 presents X-ray diffraction (XRD) peak profile analysis and dislocation density evaluation for Fe-6.8Al ferritic low-density steel under three processing conditions: 98% cold rolling (CR), and isothermal annealing at 750 °C for 5 min and 60 min. Panel (a) shows normalized intensity of the (110) diffraction peak, where the 98%CR sample exhibits significant peak broadening relative to the annealed states, and the inset displays full XRD patterns confirming ferrite phase peaks indexed as (110), (200), (211), (220), and (310). Panels (b), (c), and (d) plot the second-order moment M₂ (solid curves) and the normalized fourth-order moment M₄/q² (dashed curves) as functions of the scattering vector q for the 98%CR, 750 °C/5 min, and 750 °C/60 min conditions, respectively, derived from fitting the (110) peak profile to separate size and strain broadening contributions. Panel (e) reports the calculated dislocation density versus annealing time, revealing a drastic drop of approximately three orders of magnitude from the heavily cold-rolled state (~10¹⁵ m⁻²) down to ~10¹² m⁻² after 60 min annealing. The collective data demonstrate that recrystallization upon annealing sharply reduces lattice micro-strain and annihilates dislocations, with most recovery occurring within the first 5 min at 750 °C.

Figure 8 presents mechanical characterization of Fe-6.8Al ferritic low-density steel, comprising (a) engineering stress–strain curves and (b) rate of strain hardening versus true strain. Subfigure (a) compares three conditions: 98% cold rolling (CR), and isothermal annealing at 750 °C for 5 min and 60 min. The 98% CR specimen exhibits an ultra-high yield strength near 1800 MPa but fractures at approximately 1.2% engineering strain. After annealing for 5 min, the yield strength drops to about 700 MPa with an ultimate tensile strength of ~870 MPa and uniform elongation of ~20%. Extending annealing to 60 min further reduces the yield strength to ~265 MPa and ultimate tensile strength to ~394 MPa, while elongation increases to ~29.8%. Subfigure (b) shows that the 60 min annealed sample possesses a markedly higher initial strain hardening rate than the 5 min sample; both curves decline with increasing true strain and converge toward zero near a true strain of ~0.15–0.20. These results demonstrate the pronounced strength–ductility trade-off controlled by recovery, recrystallization, and grain growth during annealing, with nano-sized ordered precipitates contributing to sustained work hardening in the 5 min condition.

This bar graph quantifies the individual contributions of five strengthening mechanisms—dislocations, grain boundaries, ordering, solid solution, and lattice frictional stress—to the yield strength (YS) of Fe-6.8Al ferritic low-density steel under three processing conditions: 98% cold rolling (CR), and isothermal annealing at 750 °C for 5 min and 60 min. The 98% CR state exhibits the highest YS of 1800 MPa, dominated by dislocation strengthening and grain boundary hardening. After annealing at 750 °C for 5 min, the YS drops to 700 MPa, primarily due to a drastic reduction in dislocation density, while contributions from grain boundaries and nano-sized ordered Fe3Al (DO3) phases remain significant. Prolonged annealing for 60 min further reduces the YS to 265 MPa, as all strengthening contributions diminish substantially owing to grain growth and extended recovery. The comparison demonstrates that the ultrahigh strength in the cold-rolled condition arises mainly from work-hardening via dislocations, whereas controlled short-term annealing retains useful strength through grain boundary and ordered-phase strengthening while improving ductility.
